3 min read
Just now

--

A dashboard doesn’t create value because it looks impressive.
It creates value because it helps someone make a better decision, faster.
Unfortunately, many dashboards become collections of charts instead of decision-support systems. They display data but fail to communicate insight. As a result, leaders spend more time interpreting visuals than making decisions.
Here are some of the most common dashboard design mistakes I see and how to avoid them.
1. Starting with Charts Instead of Questions
One of the biggest mistakes is opening Power BI or Tableau and immediately creating visualizations.
The first question should never be “Which chart should I use?”
Instead, ask:

• What decision will this dashboard support?

• Who is the audience?

• What actions should this dashboard enable?
A dashboard should answer business questions, not simply visualize available data.
2. Measuring Everything Instead of What Matters
Many dashboards contain dozens of KPIs because teams assume that more information is better.
In reality, every additional metric increases cognitive load.
High-performing dashboards focus on a small number of meaningful metrics that directly align with business objectives.
Ask yourself:
If I removed this metric, would anyone make a different decision?
If the answer is no, it probably doesn’t belong.
3. Using the Wrong Visual for the Data
Visualizations are not interchangeable.
Choosing the wrong chart often creates misleading interpretations.
Examples include:

• Pie charts with too many categories

• Stacked bar charts for precise comparisons

• Dual-axis charts without clear relationships

• 3D charts that distort perception

• Gauges that consume space while conveying little information
The goal is not visual creativity.
The goal is accurate communication.
4. Ignoring Visual Hierarchy
Human attention follows patterns.
If every chart has the same size, color intensity, and emphasis, users struggle to identify what matters most.
Effective dashboards establish hierarchy through:

• Larger visuals for critical KPIs

• Consistent spacing

• Strategic use of color

• Clear grouping of related information

• Minimal distractions
The most important insight should be obvious within the first few seconds.
5. Overusing Colors
Color should communicate meaning — not decorate the dashboard.
When every visual uses different colors, nothing stands out.
A better approach is to use a neutral palette and reserve strong colors for exceptions:

• Green for targets achieved

• Red for issues requiring attention

• Amber for warnings
Intentional color choices improve comprehension.
6. Forgetting Context
A KPI without context rarely drives action.
Imagine seeing:
Revenue: ₹4.2 Crore
Is that good?
Impossible to know.
Now compare it with:

• Last month

• Last year

• Target

• Forecast

• Industry benchmark
Numbers become meaningful only when users understand what they should compare them against.
7. Prioritizing Aesthetics Over Performance
Animations, custom visuals, high-resolution images, and complex calculations may improve appearance but often reduce usability.
Slow dashboards discourage adoption.
Users value responsiveness more than decorative elements.
Performance is part of good design.
8. Building for Everyone
Executives, sales managers, analysts, finance teams, and operations leaders all require different information.
A single dashboard rarely satisfies every audience.
Instead, design dashboards around specific user personas and decision-making needs.
Good dashboards are audience-specific, not universal.
9. Hiding the Story in the Data
Dashboards should answer three questions:

• What happened?

• Why did it happen?

• What should we do next?
If users leave with only the first answer, the dashboard has delivered information — not insight.
The most valuable dashboards guide action.
10. Designing Without User Feedback
Many dashboards are considered “finished” once published.
In reality, dashboard development is iterative.
Observe how users interact with it.
Notice where they hesitate.
Identify which filters they ignore.
Understand which visuals they return to repeatedly.
Continuous refinement creates dashboards people actually use.
Final Thought
A beautiful dashboard that fails to improve decisions is simply digital artwork.
A great dashboard reduces uncertainty.
It highlights what matters.
It reveals opportunities.
It exposes risks.
And most importantly, it helps people make confident business decisions.
Because dashboards are not built to display data.
They are built to support better decisions.
With Appreciation,
Thanusree Gajendran
